source_intake_record.raw_question_text: Figure shows the essentials of a mass spectrometer, which can be used to measure the mass of an ion; an ion of mass \( m \) (to be measured) and charge \( q \) is produced in source \( S \). The initially stationary ion is accelerated by the electric field due to a potential difference \( V \). The ion leaves \( S \) and enters a separator chamber in which a uniform magnetic field \( \vec{B} \) is perpendicular to the path of the ion. A wide detector lines the bottom wall of the chamber, and the \( \vec{B} \) causes the ion to move in a semicircle and thus strike the detector. Suppose that \( B = 80.000\ \mathrm{mT} \), \( V = 1000.0\ \mathrm{V} \), and ions of charge \( q = +1.6022 \times 10^{-19}\ \mathrm{C} \) strike the detector at a point that lies at \( x = 1.6254\ \mathrm{m} \). What is the mass \( m \) of the individual ions, in atomic mass units (Eq. 1-7: 1 u = \( 1.6605 \times 10^{-27}\ \mathrm{kg} \))?

image_dependency: question explicitly begins with 'Figure shows' and refers to the depicted spectrometer layout
solving appears to depend on spatial/geometry information from the figure (semicircle path and detector hit position x)
asset registry shows an available primary image and registry_passed=true
multi_step: requires combining at least two physics relations: acceleration through potential difference V and circular motion in magnetic field B
problem uses multiple given quantities (B, V, q, x) plus unit conversion from kg to atomic mass units
likely needs figure-text integration to map the detector position x to orbit geometry before computing mass
verifiability: answer is a specific numeric value with units ('203.93 u')
multiple-choice options are provided, giving an additional exact check target
all needed constants and numerical inputs are explicitly supplied in the prompt

risk_flags: final value may be sensitive to rounding because several high-precision inputs are given
geometry interpretation may depend on the figure, especially how x maps to the semicircle diameter/radius

decision_reasons: Question, primary image, and answer are all present, and the asset registry passed with no recorded issues.
Initial scoring is strong overall, with high image dependency, multi-step reasoning, and verifiability scores.
The sample appears fully recoverable and usable as a figure-dependent physics problem.
Although there are minor risks around rounding and figure geometry interpretation, the core semantics are intact and well supported by the available assets.
